career.notes: Owned and led A.S. Badger Lumber Company in Chicago.

narrative_blocks.header: Wed an Oregon-born woman who lived to ninety-one
narrative_blocks.body: Edwin married Helen Jesse 'Holly' Pope, born 1892 in Portland Oregon, who survived him by twenty years and lived to ninety-one. Their two children both served in World War II — son Edwin Jr. as a US Army Air Corps Major who landed on Iwo Jima, daughter Mary Frances as a Red Cross worker in the Pacific Theater.

notable.primary_url_label: Find a Grave: Rev. Edwin Hunt Badger Jr.

education.notes: Member of Delta Kappa Epsilon Fraternity.
Ordained priest in the Episcopal Church after graduation.

education.degree: Ed.D. / Ph.D. Education and Political Science
education.notes: Doctorate in Education and Political Science.

career.title: Episcopal Priest
career.notes: Ordained 1950 after Seabury-Western Seminary B.D. Served on multiple diocesan committees, boards, and councils.

career.title: Priest-in-Charge then Rector, St. Giles Church
career.organization: St. Giles Episcopal Church, Northbrook IL
career.notes: Began ministry as Priest-in-Charge; later appointed Rector.

career.title: Dean of Ohio University Chillicothe Campus
career.notes: Headed the Chillicothe regional campus for 12 years following Indiana University doctorate.

career.title: Supply priest
career.organization: St. Paul's Episcopal Church Chillicothe and other Diocese of Southern Ohio parishes
career.notes: Continued as supply priest in retirement.

military_service.notes: 22 months in the Western Pacific combat area. LANDED ON IWO JIMA on the FIFTH DAY of the battle (one of the bloodiest of the Pacific Theater). Retired as Major in 1946.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousin who died nursing for France in WWI
Cleveland Leader publisher granduncle, his Edwin namesake

narrative_blocks.header: Iwo Jima on the fifth day of battle
narrative_blocks.body: A US Army Air Corps officer, Edwin Jr. spent twenty-two months in the Western Pacific combat area during World War II. He landed on Iwo Jima on the fifth day of the battle — one of the bloodiest engagements of the Pacific Theater — and retired in 1946 with the rank of Major.

narrative_blocks.header: Episcopal priest at St. Giles Northbrook 1950-1966
narrative_blocks.body: After Seabury-Western Theological Seminary in 1950, Edwin Jr. was ordained as an Episcopal priest in the Diocese of Chicago. He served as Priest-in-Charge then Rector at St. Giles Church in Northbrook IL from 1950 through 1966 — sixteen years at one parish — and on numerous diocesan committees and councils.

narrative_blocks.header: Dean of Ohio University Chillicothe 1969-1981
narrative_blocks.body: After three Indiana University degrees in adult education and political science, culminating in a 1969 doctorate, Edwin Jr. headed Ohio University's Chillicothe regional campus as Dean for twelve years. He continued as a supply priest in the Diocese of Southern Ohio after retiring.

narrative_blocks.header: Eagle Scout and Silver Beaver Award honoree
narrative_blocks.body: A lifelong Boy Scouts of America supporter, Edwin Jr. attained the rank of Eagle Scout and was later presented with the Silver Beaver Award for his service to the Chief Logan Council. He was also a Paul Harris Fellow of Rotary International through the Chillicothe Rotary Club.

burial.plot_notes: Christ Church Churchyard, Winnetka — Bridewell family plot with husband and both sons

notable.notable_blurb: WWII Red Cross Pacific veteran; Planned Parenthood activist
notable.primary_url_label: Find a Grave: Mary Frances Badger Bridewell

career.title: WWII American Red Cross worker
career.notes: Served country during World War II as a member of the American Red Cross in the South Pacific Theater — including GUADALCANAL, GUAM, and OKINAWA.

career.title: Traveling Secretary
career.notes: After the war, served as traveling secretary for her Alpha Phi sorority.

career.title: Civic activist + board member
career.notes: Active with and on the boards of: Chicago Junior League; Community Center of Chinatown; Consultation for Epilepsy; Infant Welfare Society; Fortnightly Society; Planned Parenthood/Chicago Area.

military_service.notes: Served the South Pacific Theater of WWII including Guadalcanal, Guam, and Okinawa.

cross_connections.display_label: Croix-Rouge WWI cousin who preceded her by a generation

narrative_blocks.header: Red Cross worker on Guadalcanal, Guam, Okinawa
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Frances served her country during World War II as a member of the American Red Cross in the South Pacific Theater — including the assault grounds of Guadalcanal, Guam, and Okinawa. Her brother Edwin Jr. was simultaneously serving in the same theater as a US Army Air Corps Major.

narrative_blocks.header: Board member, Planned Parenthood and Junior League
narrative_blocks.body: After the war, Mary Frances served as traveling secretary for her Alpha Phi sorority, then settled into civic activism. She served on the boards of the Chicago Junior League, Community Center of Chinatown, Consultation for Epilepsy, Infant Welfare Society, the Fortnightly Society, and Planned Parenthood/Chicago Area.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived two of her sons before she died
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Frances was predeceased by her husband David in 1999 and by both of her sons — Jonathan in 1989 and Alexander in 2004. Her sister-in-law Polly Heaslett, who served the same Pacific Theater Red Cross with whom Mary's brother Edwin Jr. was stationed, also outlived her.

career.title: Civic volunteer + tutor
career.organization: Chicago St. Edmund's Academy + Carpenter Public School
career.notes: With wife Anne (I00423), served as a volunteer tutor and Board member at Chicago's St. Edmund's Academy and a tutor at Chicago's Carpenter Public School.

cross_connections.display_label: Cleveland Leader publisher great-grand-uncle, Cowles surname
Tribune business manager great-grand-uncle, Cowles surname

narrative_blocks.header: Third sibling of an Iwo Jima priest
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel was the third child of Edwin Hunt Badger Sr. and Helen Pope, after Episcopal priest and Iwo Jima veteran Edwin Jr. (born 1916) and Red Cross worker Mary Frances Bridewell (born 1919). He married Anne Howell Redmon in 1958 — fifty-two years of marriage — and named his son Samuel Cowles Badger Jr., carrying the Cowles maternal name into a fifth Chicago generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried the Cowles surname into five generations
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel was given the Cowles surname as his middle name from his great-grandmother Sarah Frances Cowles Badger of the San Francisco Cowles judicial family. He passed it to his son Samuel Cowles Badger Jr., extending the Cowles thread that began with their great-great-grandfather Dr. Edwin Weed Cowles of Austinburg, Ohio.

sources.short: Edwin Hunt Badger Jr 2005 obituary (per HD3753 sources)
Mary Frances Bridewell 2008 obituary (per HD3754 sources)
Anne Howell Redmon Badger 2010 obituary (per I00423 sources)

death.cause: died at Glen Oaks Nursing Home, Northbrook

burial.plot_notes: Fairlawn Section Block 1, Graceland Cemetery — GPS 41.9587253, -87.6602329 — buried with parents and siblings

notable.notable_blurb: WPA muralist; Chicago Society of Artists president

education.institution: School of the Art Institute of Chicago
education.notes: Began at age 6 at the Winnetka branch under Pauline Dohn Rudolph.

education.institution: School of the Art Institute of Chicago
education.notes: Studied with Matilda Vanderpoel and muralist John Warner Norton.

education.institution: School of the Art Institute of Chicago

career.title: Muralist
career.organization: Independent / Roycemore School / Fine Arts Building studio
career.notes: After 1925 European study trip with Matilda Vanderpoel, took studio at Chicago's Fine Arts Building and taught art at Roycemore School.

career.title: WPA Illinois Art Project muralist
career.organization: Works Progress Administration / Illinois Art Project
career.notes: Created murals for libraries, schools, hospitals, and field houses in Chicago, Oak Park, and Joliet IL after the Great Depression wiped out her family's fortune. Notable WPA murals: Joliet Township High School (1928, 1930); Audy Home (Chicago — Treasure Island themes, recently rediscovered); Cook County Children's Hospital; Hall of Science at the 1933 Century of Progress exhibition; Stevenson playground in Oak Park (Oak Park murals preserved by Oak Park Historical Society).

career.title: Exhibitor, Art Institute of Chicago
career.notes: 17 annual exhibitions at AIC between 1927 and 1943. Most celebrated work: Celery Pickers, No. 1 (1941 Chicago and Vicinity Exhibition); companion Celery Pickers, No. 2 (1943 International Watercolor Exhibition).

career.title: President, Chicago Society of Artists
career.notes: Held the presidency in 1942.

career.title: Studio mate at Tree Studios
career.notes: Shared working space with Rue Wintherbotham Shaw, president of the Arts Club of Chicago.

career.title: Art teacher
career.organization: School of the Art Institute of Chicago
career.notes: Returned to teach at SAIC in the 1940s alongside friends Ethel Spears and Kathleen Blackshear.

narrative_blocks.header: Chicago Society of Artists president in 1942
narrative_blocks.body: An indispensable presence in Chicago's interwar art community, Frances served as president of the Chicago Society of Artists in 1942. She was an active member of the Arts Club of Chicago and shared studio space at the Tree Studios in the mid 1930s with Rue Wintherbotham Shaw, the Arts Club's president.

narrative_blocks.header: WPA muralist after the family fortune collapsed
narrative_blocks.body: After the Great Depression wiped out the Badger family's fortune, Frances qualified for the WPA Illinois Art Project despite her socially prominent background. She painted murals for libraries, schools, hospitals, and field houses across Chicago, Oak Park, and Joliet — including the 1933 Century of Progress Hall of Science and a Treasure Island series at the Audy Home.

narrative_blocks.header: Seventeen exhibitions at the Art Institute of Chicago
narrative_blocks.body: Frances exhibited at the Art Institute of Chicago seventeen times between 1927 and 1943. Her most celebrated canvas, 'Celery Pickers No. 1' — a luminous oil depicting Black agricultural workers — appeared at the 1941 Chicago and Vicinity Exhibition. A gouache sequel followed in the 1943 International Watercolor Exhibition.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied and taught at the Art Institute herself
narrative_blocks.body: Frances began at the SAIC junior branch in Winnetka at age six and earned her studio art degree there in 1925 under Matilda Vanderpoel and John Warner Norton. She returned to teach in the 1940s alongside Ethel Spears and Kathleen Blackshear, and completed a formal B.F.A. there in 1949.

burial.plot_notes: Memorial Park Cemetery, Skokie, Cook County, Illinois (per Find a Grave; cemetery not separately added to CEM array since only Paul + his mother Flora are buried there from the Hooker tree — below 2+ threshold)

parents.mother_name: Flora Irwin Schofield (American painter; Whitney Museum collection)
parents.mother_research_notes: Built as easter_egg X02776 in Stage 6X-2.

burial.plot_notes: Cowles family plot, Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma CA

notable.notable_blurb: San Francisco police judge then county judge

education.notes: Several terms at Grand River Institute Austinburg OH.
Finished his education at Western Reserve College.

career.title: Cleveland lawyer
career.notes: Admitted to the Ohio bar 1847. Partnered first with Loren Prentiss; in 1850 parted with Prentiss to join a new enterprise with childhood friend Edwin P. Mastick. Cleveland practice focused on criminal defense (clients accused of rape, arson, insanity), real estate transactions, and a range of civil matters.

career.title: Secretary-Treasurer, Cataract Fire Company No. V
career.organization: Cataract Fire Company No. V, Cleveland
career.notes: Volunteer fire-company role during his Cleveland years.

career.title: California migration
career.notes: Migrated to San Francisco with childhood friend and law partner Edwin P. Mastick — both 'struck with California fever' two years after the diary ends.

career.title: Deputy Clerk, Supreme Court of California
career.notes: First California position upon arriving in San Francisco.

career.title: Member, San Francisco Vigilance Committee
career.organization: San Francisco Committee of Vigilance of 1856
career.notes: Active member of the 1856 Vigilance Committee — one of the largest extralegal civic-purification movements in 19th-century US history.

career.title: San Francisco Police Judge
career.notes: Elected Police Judge — also one of three commissioners selecting the SF police force during a period of civic reform. (Earlier project notes had this as 1856; correct dates per the new Carpe Librum biographical source are 1860-1863.)

career.title: County Judge of San Francisco
career.notes: Presided over the SF County Court for four years; recorded decisions form a significant chapter in California jurisprudence.

career.title: Member, Committee of Safety
career.notes: Active during the 1877 SF railroad strikes.

career.title: President, Savings and Loan Society (Clay Street Bank)
career.organization: Savings and Loan Society, San Francisco
career.notes: Long-time legal adviser to the Clay Street Bank; elected its president 1879. Held the post until his Nov 17, 1880 death.

career.title: Senior partner, Cowles & Brown
career.notes: Returned to private SF practice 1868; partnership with Albert N. Brown until his 1880 death.

narrative_blocks.header: San Francisco police then county judge 1860-1868
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel was elected San Francisco Police Judge in 1860 — also one of three commissioners selecting the SF police force — and served the County Court bench from 1863 to 1868. His recorded decisions form a significant chapter in California jurisprudence during the state's formative years.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of San Francisco's 1856 Vigilance Committee
narrative_blocks.body: An earnest and efficient worker in 'the great social purification' of San Francisco, Samuel served the 1856 Vigilance Committee — one of the largest extralegal civic movements in 19th-century US history. He gloried in those days and rehearsed the story in later life.

narrative_blocks.header: His Cleveland diary documents the 1849 cholera epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel kept a clear daily diary from July 1849 through July 1850 in Cleveland, recording the cholera epidemic that he himself contracted and survived; an hour-by-hour account of President Zachary Taylor's death; steamboat-fire disasters; and his own self-recorded depression. The diary survives as an important primary source for early Cleveland history.

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated to California with childhood friend Mastick
narrative_blocks.body: Struck with 'California fever,' Samuel left Cleveland in 1852 with his childhood friend and law partner Edwin P. Mastick. He had been preceded by his brother Edwin Cowles' departure for Chicago — both sons of Dr. Edwin Weed Cowles fanning out from Austinburg Ohio in the same gold-rush decade.

sources.short: History of Ashtabula Co., Ohio (biographical sketch)

burial.plot_notes: Cowles family plot, Cypress Lawn — buried with husband Hon. Samuel Cowles

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #687 — Dr. Edwin Weed Cowles
History of Ashtabula Co., Ohio (biographical sketch + portrait)

notable.notable_blurb: Cleveland physician (1794-1861) renowned for his courage during

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow abolitionist + Underground Railroad operator across the Ohio-Connecticut axis.
Fellow Hooker-descendant abolitionist of the antebellum Northern circuit.

education.notes: Early education from his father Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles and at the Farmington Academy.

education.institution: Studied with Dr. O.K. Hawley, Austinburg OH
education.notes: Medical apprenticeship under Dr. O.K. Hawley of Austinburg Ohio.

career.title: Physician
career.notes: Practiced Austinburg OH ~1815-1830, Mantua OH ~1830-1832, Cleveland from 1832, Ann Arbor + Detroit MI 1834-1838, Cleveland again until death.

career.title: Cholera-epidemic physician
career.notes: During Cleveland's first cholera epidemic, when most residents fled, Cowles stayed administering to the sick day and night. When an entire steamboat crew became cholera victims, he boarded the ship and stayed until everything possible had been done.

career.title: Underground Railroad superintendent
career.notes: Member of the 'Liberty Guard' and Superintendent of the Underground Railroad in Cleveland. 'Many a poor fugitive slave owed his freedom to his kind offices and exertions while in this position.'

career.title: Liberty Party
career.notes: Joined Liberty Party 1841 (after supporting Gen. Harrison 1840 as old-line Whig).

career.title: Free-Soil Party
career.notes: Joined Free-Soil Party in 1848.

career.title: Republican Party
career.notes: Joined Republican Party at its founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Stayed during Cleveland's cholera epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: When a fatal cholera epidemic struck Cleveland during Edwin Weed Cowles' first year of residence in 1832, most of the city fled. Disregarding family pleas, Cowles stayed at his post — even boarding a steamboat where the whole crew had collapsed — treating the stricken day and night. His reputation for fearlessness and benevolence was made in that first year and lasted his lifetime.

narrative_blocks.header: Underground Railroad superintendent in Cleveland
narrative_blocks.body: Cowles served as superintendent of the Underground Railroad in Cleveland through the Liberty Guard. He held that slavery violated every commandment of the decalogue and every principle of justice. His political evolution went Whig 1840, Liberty Party 1841, Free-Soil 1848, and Republican 1855.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited from his abolitionist half-brother in Cleveland
narrative_blocks.body: When Edwin's older half-brother Samuel Cowles (1775-1837) — Cleveland Court of Common Pleas judge and 1827 president of the Cuyahoga County Colonization Society — died childless in 1837, he left his estate to Edwin's children. Samuel had practiced law in Farmington and Hartford before emigrating to Cleveland in 1819, carrying the same abolitionist current that ran in Edwin's own Underground Railroad work.

bio.extra_names: Family habitually spelled 'Foot' rather than the modern 'Foote'.
bio.bio_blurb: Doctor’s wife of an old Wethersfield Foote line

parents.father_name: Asa Foote (Norfolk CT and Salina NY)
parents.mother_name: Sarah Mills Cowles Foote (widow of Joseph Cowles #429)
parents.father_research_notes: Asa Foote (Ephraim⁵, Ephraim⁴, Nathaniel³, Nathaniel², Nathaniel¹) of Norfolk CT and Salina NY. Direct line back to Nathaniel¹ Foote settler at Watertown MA 1633 and later Wethersfield CT (d.1644) — Nathaniel¹'s widow Elizabeth Deming Foote remarried Gov. Thomas Welles. Not built per Jalapeño rule.
parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Mills Cowles Foote (Dea. Joseph³ Mills, John², Simeon¹/Simon¹) — widow of Joseph Cowles #429 of Norfolk CT. Through her first marriage Almira's half-siblings include Joseph Brooks Cowles #1037 of Austinburg OH and Samuel Cowles #1038 of Cleveland OH. Not built per Jalapeño rule.

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow Hooker-tree spouse from the Nathaniel¹ Foote 1635 line.
Fellow Hooker-tree spouse from the Nathaniel¹ Foote 1635 line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her great-grandfather fought at the Falls Fight
narrative_blocks.body: Three generations back, her ancestor Nathaniel Foote stood in the Falls Fight at Turner’s Falls — the brutal 1676 dawn raid of King Philip’s War. She married a doctor, bore six children, and carried that Wethersfield-founding line west to Cleveland, where she died in 1846.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #687 — Dr. Edwin Weed Cowles

burialCemetery.name: Graceland Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Founded 1860 Chicago. Hooker-tree connections wired in 6W-2 + 6W-4: Sarah Frances Cowles Badger family plot in Fairlawn Section.
